name: Effectful Object Calculi
summary: Dagnino, Giannini, and Zucca (ECOOP 2025): studies objects in the presence of explicit effects. Method calls, object identity, field access, inheritance-like dispatch, and exception-like control all interact with type-and-effect reasoning. The lesson: effects should be designed into the object core, not only added to function types.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Dagnino, Giannini, and Zucca's **An Effectful Object Calculus** (ECOOP 2025) studies objects in the presence of explicit effects. The design point is the interaction between object-oriented constructs — method calls, object identity, field access, inheritance-like dispatch, and exception-like control — and type-and-effect reasoning.

Object calculi are often used as small cores for class-based or prototype-based languages; making effects explicit in such a core gives a place to state and prove which operations may allocate, mutate, throw, perform I/O, or interact with handlers.

sections.title: Design lesson — effects in the object core
sections.content:
The lesson: **effects should be designed into the object core, not only added to function types**. Languages with methods, mutable objects, and effect annotations need rules for how receiver effects, method-body effects, overridden-method effects, and object initialization effects compose.

The practical engineering implication is that **effect annotations on methods need to interact correctly with subtype polymorphism**: an overriding method must declare an effect set compatible with the overridden method, and the dispatch rule must respect the contravariant-in-argument-effects, covariant-in-result-effects discipline. Languages that retrofit effects onto an existing OO type system tend to get this wrong on first attempt; an effectful object calculus designed up front avoids the retrofit traps.
